title: Pyxidaria elata
intro: Pyxidaria elata, commonly known as the "Saraca indica" or "Ashoka tree," is a tropical plant species native to Southeast Asia, characterized by its unique, long, and straight flowers that resemble a pyramid. The Ashoka tree has been used in traditional medicine for centuries to treat various health issues, including menstrual disorders, inflammation, and digestive problems, due to its potential health benefits such as regulating menstrual cycles and reducing pain. The bioactive constituents of Pyxidaria elata include flavonoids, alkaloids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Pyxidaria elata is commonly used to make various preparations, such as teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as being used in Ayurvedic medicine to create herbal formulations like Ashoka capsules or Ashoka powder.
taxonomy: Pyxidaria elata is classified as a species of fungus within the order Pyxidariales and the family Pyxidariaceae. It belongs to the phylum Ascomycota, which is a large group of fungi that includes over 64,000 known species. Pyxidaria elata is further classified into the genus Pyxidaria, which is a monotypic genus containing only this species. The classification of Pyxidaria elata is based on its unique characteristics and morphological features. This classification helps to identify and understand the evolutionary relationships among different species within the fungal kingdom.
